args_redacted.query: zero trust architecture NIST principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the traditional
  network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system should be
  trusted by default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect information assets. If one layer fails, the next layer
  continues to provide protection. It is based on the military concept of
  creating multiple barriers that an attacker must breach.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never Trust, Always Verify — Every access request is authenticated,
    authorized, and encrypted before granting access.
  • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems receive only the minimum
    level of access needed to perform their function.
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised;
    minimize blast radius and segment access.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Divide the network into small, isolated zones;
    lateral movement is severely restricted.
  • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — User and device trust is
    continuously reassessed; no session is trusted indefinitely.
  • Explicit Verification — Use all available data points (identity,
    location, device health, service, data classification) to make
    access decisions.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Protection — Deploy multiple security controls at different
    levels (physical, network, host, application, data).
  • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy across controls ensures that
    the failure of one layer does not result in total compromise.
  • Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter-Based Foundation — Traditionally relies on a strong outer
    perimeter (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with internal layers behind it.
  • Depth Over Breadth — Focus on stacking controls deeply rather than
    spreading them thinly across the environment.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Core Philosophy             | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers compensate for the        |
|                             | verify everything, trust nothing | weaknesses of individual controls|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | Network is hostile; no implicit  | Internal network is safer than   |
|                             | trust based on network location  | external; perimeter matters      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity-centric; who you are    | Control-centric; where you are   |
|                             | matters more than where you are  | and what layers protect you      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Reliance          | Perimeter is irrelevant; every   | Strong perimeter is the first    |
|                             | resource is externally-facing    | and most critical layer          |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via micro-    | Slowed but not always prevented; |
|                             | segmentation and least privilege | relies on internal layers        |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Model                | Per-session, per-resource,       | Often session-based with broader |
|                             | continuously validated           | access once inside the perimeter |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting  | Moderate — can be built          |
|                             | identity, network, and apps      | incrementally on existing infra  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Historical Context          | Modern (formalized ~2010 by      | Established (military concept    |
|                             | Forrester; NIST SP 800-207)      | adapted to infosec in 1990s-2000s)|
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+


4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are not mutually exclusive. In fact, they work best
when combined:
